title: ZKP-Based Agent Authentication to Prevent Credential Theft
description: OpenAI's Codex agent flaw exposed GitHub passwords by passing external text into OS commands. Bolyra's ZKP handshake eliminates long-lived credentials entirely—agents prove identity via zero-knowledge proofs without exposing secrets, making credential theft attacks structurally impossible. This is a direct fit for the GitGuardian-identified pattern of shared tokens across agents.
source: OpenAI Codex credential theft (bankinfosecurity) + GitGuardian agent auth blog

rationale.demand: The OpenAI Codex credential theft and GitGuardian blog are real documented incidents, but web search evidence returned zero results, so only 2 named sources exist with no verified URLs, engagement metrics, or named buyers actively seeking ZKP-based CI auth.
rationale.timing: Agent security incidents are increasing and the problem is real, but no competitor has shipped a ZKP-based CI credential solution, no standard body is drafting ZKP CI auth profiles, and no regulatory deadline forces adoption — the window is open but not closing.
rationale.fit: Excellent primitive mapping: AgentPolicy for credential proofs, HumanUniqueness for operator accountability, Delegation for sub-agent scope narrowing, and IdentityRegistry for nullifier replay protection all apply directly with no new circuits needed.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is well-scoped at ~10 days using existing primitives with no circuit changes, though GitHub Action adoption requires convincing teams to replace familiar PAT workflows with a novel ZKP-based approach that has no ecosystem precedent.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive. Agent proves it holds a valid operator-signed credential with sufficient permissions (e.g., WRITE_DATA for git push) without revealing the credential commitment, operator key, or full permission bitmask. Selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets a CI gateway verify 'this agent may push code' without learning what else the agent is authorized to do. Expiry enforcement ensures short-lived proof windows even if a proof artifact leaks.

Operator accountability. The human who issued the agent credential proves enrollment in the operator tree, binding a real human identity to every agent deployment. Nullifier scoped to the CI platform prevents one operator from spawning unlimited unaccountable agents within the same service context. This directly addresses GitGuardian's 'shared tokens across agents' pattern — each agent traces to exactly one human without revealing who.

Scope-narrowed sub-delegation. When Codex-style agents spawn sub-agents or tool-use chains, delegation ensures each hop can only narrow permissions (e.g., agent with WRITE_DATA delegates READ_DATA-only to a linting sub-agent). The 3-hop cap prevents unbounded delegation chains. Expiry narrowing means sub-agent credentials expire before the parent — leaked sub-agent proofs have a strictly shorter attack window.

On-chain anchor for handshake verification and nullifier replay protection. A CI/CD gateway or API proxy calls verifyHandshake atomically — if the agent's proof is replayed (stolen and resubmitted), the nullifier check rejects it. Root history buffers (30 entries) tolerate Merkle tree updates during CI pipeline latency without invalidating in-flight proofs.

createAgentCredential() issues the credential, proveHandshake() generates the ZKP pair before each API call (replacing token auth), verifyHandshake() runs server-side. The existing error hierarchy (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, StaleProofError) already models the failure modes of this use case.

new_components_needed.description: GitHub App / CI gateway adapter: an HTTP proxy or GitHub App that intercepts API calls from agents, replaces Bearer token auth with a Bolyra proveHandshake() flow, and verifies the ZKP server-side before forwarding. Essentially a drop-in replacement for PATs/OAuth tokens in CI pipelines.

Credential rotation helper: a lightweight SDK function that re-issues agent credentials with fresh expiry windows on a schedule (e.g., per-CI-run), so there are never long-lived credentials to steal. Wraps createAgentCredential() with monotonic expiry and operator re-signing.

IETF profile or application note for 'ZKP-based agent authentication in CI/CD pipelines' — maps draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 flows onto concrete GitHub/GitLab/Jenkins integration points, defining the trust boundaries (where proving happens vs. where verification happens).

OpenAI Codex / Agents SDK adapter: a thin wrapper that hooks into the OpenAI agent tool-use lifecycle, calling proveHandshake() before any external API call and attaching the proof as a request header instead of a raw token.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Bolyra doesn't compete with GitHub, OpenAI, or GitGuardian — it sits underneath them as the authentication primitive. GitHub keeps its UI and API surface; OpenAI keeps its agent runtime; GitGuardian keeps its scanning product. Bolyra replaces the token-based auth layer with ZKP-based auth, invisible to end users. The Codex incident creates urgency (timing 23/25) because incumbents need to patch credential handling NOW and building ZKP infrastructure from scratch is a 12+ month effort they won't undertake. Bolyra can be the drop-in privacy layer they adopt rather than build.

deliverables.name: ci-credential-helpers
deliverables.description: Thin wrapper in @bolyra/sdk: createCICredential(operatorKey, repoScope, ttlSeconds) and verifyCIProof(proof, expectedRepo). Maps git push/pull/read to existing permission bits. TTL defaults to 300s (single CI run). No circuit changes—just ergonomic sugar over createAgentCredential + proveHandshake.

deliverables.name: bolyra-github-action
deliverables.description: GitHub Action that wraps git operations. On workflow start: calls createCICredential for the job's declared scope, generates a ZKP per API call via proveHandshake(), passes proof as a custom header. Companion verify endpoint (Express middleware, ~100 LOC) checks proof server-side. Replaces PAT/GITHUB_TOKEN for Bolyra-aware services. Ships as a public action in the repo.

deliverables.name: credential-theft-demo
deliverables.description: Reproducible demo: two GitHub Actions workflows in an example repo. Workflow A uses a PAT (shows how env dump leaks it). Workflow B uses Bolyra ZKP (shows proof is useless after TTL, replay fails via nullifier). README walks through the attack vector and mitigation. No live secrets—uses a mock target service.

deliverables.name: ci-auth-spec-addendum
deliverables.description: Appendix to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01: 'CI/CD Agent Authentication Profile'. Defines how sessionNonce binds to (repo, workflow_run_id, job_id), recommended TTL ranges, and scope-to-permission-bit mapping for git operations. Normative enough to be an IETF-style appendix.

deliverables.name: technical-blog-post
deliverables.description: 'How ZKP handshakes eliminate credential theft in CI agents'—educational post walking through the Codex vulnerability, why token-based auth is structurally broken for agents, and how Bolyra's approach works. Links to the demo repo. No pricing, no CTA, purely technical.

risks: GitHub Actions marketplace review may flag the action as security-sensitive, slowing publication
No real enterprise demand signal yet—web search failed, so the 'credential theft in agents' narrative may be a niche concern rather than a buying trigger
Server-side verify endpoint requires the target service to adopt Bolyra—chicken-and-egg problem without a first integration partner
TTL-based proofs add latency (~200ms snarkjs, ~15ms rapidsnark) per git operation; CI pipelines are latency-sensitive
Solo-founder bandwidth: 11 days is optimistic if circuit compile issues or GitHub Action sandboxing edge cases surface
mvp.success_criteria: The demo repo's two workflows run green in GitHub Actions: Workflow A visibly leaks the PAT in logs, Workflow B completes git push with ZKP auth and a replayed proof is rejected. At least one external developer (not the author) can clone the demo and run it without assistance. The spec addendum passes a self-review against RFC 2119 keyword usage.

title: Mutual ZKP Authentication for Human-Agent Interactions
description: Multiple signals show agent-to-service auth, but none address the human-in-the-loop verification gap. Bolyra's mutual handshake protocol (HumanUniqueness + AgentPolicy) uniquely proves both that the human is a real enrolled member AND the agent has valid scoped credentials—solving the 'who authorized this agent?' audit problem enterprises face.
source: GitHub community discussion on AI security headaches + GitGuardian blog

rationale.demand: Web search evidence completely failed with zero verified URLs; only two unlinked source mentions (GitHub discussion, GitGuardian blog) land this squarely in anecdotal territory.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were verified; timing assessment returned 18+ months with no concrete anchors.
rationale.fit: This is Bolyra's canonical use case — all 5 named primitives (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, IdentityRegistry, Delegation, SDK handshake API) map directly with at most minor modification, and the mutual auth flow already ships as a 4-line SDK call.
rationale.feasibility: MVP spec is tightly scoped at ~10 days across 5 deliverables that reuse existing circuits and SDK, with only a minor Delegation circuit change required.

mapped_primitives.usage: Proves the human is a real enrolled member via Semaphore v4 Merkle membership + nullifier. The scope-bound nullifier gives sybil resistance per service context, and nonce binding prevents replay. This is the 'who authorized this agent?' anchor — the human side of mutual auth.

Proves the agent holds a valid, non-expired credential with scoped permissions. Selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets the verifier check 'does this agent have FINANCIAL_SMALL?' without learning the full bitmask. The operator EdDSA signature ties the credential to a known operator. This is the agent side of mutual auth.

Atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) already enforces nonce equality between human and agent proofs, proving they were generated for the same session. Root history buffers handle clock skew between proof generation and on-chain verification. This is the on-chain audit trail enterprises need.

Enables the 'human delegated to agent' provenance chain that enterprises want for audit. A human can delegate a scoped subset of permissions to an agent, and the agent can sub-delegate to tool-agents — all with one-way scope narrowing enforced in-circuit, not just policy. Max 3 hops covers human → orchestrator → tool-agent.

proveHandshake() and verifyHandshake() already implement the full mutual auth flow in 4 lines of code. createHumanIdentity + createAgentCredential set up the two sides. Error classes (ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, StaleProofError) map directly to enterprise audit failure modes.

The IETF internet-draft already specifies the wire format for mutual ZKP auth, giving enterprises a standards-track reference they can point to in compliance reviews. This is a differentiator against ad-hoc agent auth schemes.

DID method provides the identifier layer: human DIDs resolve to Merkle roots, agent DIDs resolve to credential commitments. Enterprise identity systems (Azure AD, Okta) can federate via DID resolution without touching ZKP internals.

new_components_needed.description: Enterprise audit log adapter — a verifyHandshake() post-hook that emits structured audit events (JSON/OTEL) containing nullifier hash, permission scope proved, delegation chain depth, and verification timestamp. Enterprises need this for SOC 2 / SOX audit trails without exposing ZKP internals.

OIDC/OAuth2 bridge — middleware that wraps mutual ZKP auth behind a standard token endpoint. Enterprise services call a familiar /token endpoint; the bridge orchestrates proveHandshake() and returns a short-lived JWT with verified claims (human_enrolled=true, agent_scope=0x07, delegation_depth=1). This is the adoption unlock — enterprises don't rewrite their auth stack.

Enterprise deployment profile — a normative subset of draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 that specifies: required permission bits for financial vs. data-only scopes, maximum delegation depth per risk tier, nullifier rotation policy, and credential expiry bounds. Gives compliance teams a checkbox document.

Session binding middleware for MCP/A2A — adapter that injects mutual auth proofs into MCP tool calls and Google A2A task envelopes. The agent proves policy compliance per-tool-invocation, not just at session start. Maps to the @bolyra/mcp package stub.

On-chain audit registry — a lightweight contract that logs handshake verification events (nullifier, scope commitment, block timestamp) without storing PII. Enterprises query this for 'prove agent X was authorized by a human at time T' without needing the original proofs.

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical privacy-layer-under-incumbents play. Enterprises already have identity providers (Okta, Azure AD) and agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI). Bolyra doesn't replace them — it slides underneath as the verification layer that answers 'was a real human behind this agent action?' without requiring enterprises to adopt new identity infrastructure. The OIDC bridge strategy means incumbents' existing token flows gain ZKP-backed mutual auth transparently. The selective disclosure property (prove permission bits without revealing the full bitmask) maps directly to enterprise data minimization requirements under SOC 2 and GDPR. Competitors solving agent auth (OAuth-based approaches like draft-klrc, Orbs) only authenticate the agent — they have no human-side proof. This makes Bolyra the only protocol that can satisfy the 'who authorized this agent?' audit question with cryptographic certainty rather than policy assertion.

deliverables.name: Audit event emitter post-hook
deliverables.description: A verifyHandshake() post-hook in @bolyra/sdk that emits structured JSON audit events (human nullifier hash, agent credential commitment, scope mask verified, delegation depth, timestamp, session nonce). No external dependencies — just a typed EventEmitter consumers can subscribe to.

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit minor: audit metadata in public signals
deliverables.description: Add delegationDepth as a public output signal to the Delegation circuit so on-chain verifiers and audit logs can see hop count without revealing the full chain. Regenerate verifier contract + zkeys.

deliverables.name: Mutual auth end-to-end demo
deliverables.description: A single Node script (examples/mutual-auth-demo/) that walks through the full flow: enroll human, create agent credential, delegate scoped permissions, run proveHandshake, verify, and print the audit log. Serves as both a technical reference and the README example. No UI, no server — just a CLI script.

deliverables.name: Conformance test vectors for mutual auth
deliverables.description: Add mutual-auth test vectors to spec/conformance/: valid handshake, expired agent credential, scope escalation attempt, stale Merkle root, nonce mismatch, delegation depth exceeded. JSON vectors with expected pass/fail + error codes per draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.

deliverables.name: Enterprise integration guide
deliverables.description: A how-to doc (docs/enterprise-mutual-auth.md) covering: threat model (who authorized this agent?), integration pattern with existing IdPs (Azure AD/Okta via DID resolution), audit log schema reference, and 4-line SDK quickstart. Technical content only — no pricing, no CTAs.

deliverables.name: SDK error refinement for audit failure modes
deliverables.description: Ensure ExpiredCredentialError, ScopeEscalationError, StaleProofError, and new DelegationDepthExceededError all carry machine-readable codes and the failing proof's public signals — so enterprise SIEM systems can ingest them directly.

risks: Web search evidence returned nothing — demand signal is inferred from community discussions, not validated with real enterprise buyers. The 'who authorized this agent?' problem may not be acute enough yet to drive adoption.
Enterprise audit requirements vary wildly (SOC2, FedRAMP, PCI-DSS) — the generic audit event schema may not satisfy any specific compliance framework deeply enough to be useful without customization.

Delegation circuit change requires zkey regeneration — if the Groth16 ceremony artifacts drift from the deployed contract verifiers, integration tests will silently pass locally but fail on-chain. Must regenerate and redeploy verifier contracts atomically.
Solo founder capacity risk: 10 days assumes no interruptions from the other 3 autoresearch loops, patent deadline work, or ZKProva/GeniusComply maintenance.
mvp.success_criteria: The mutual-auth-demo script runs end-to-end in under 30 seconds (using rapidsnark), all 6+ conformance test vectors pass in the spec runner, the audit event emitter is documented with a working SIEM-ingestible JSON schema, and at least one external developer (GitHub issue or discussion) successfully runs the demo without needing help. Measurable proxy: 3+ GitHub stars or forks on the demo within 4 weeks of the docs/blog post announcing it.

title: One-Way Scope Narrowing for Multi-Agent Delegation Chains
description: ZeroID implements RFC 8693 token exchange for delegation chains but relies on plaintext JWT claims visible to all parties. Bolyra's Delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing cryptographically—sub-agents provably cannot escalate permissions, and the delegation chain is verifiable without revealing the full permission set. Direct replacement for token-exchange-based delegation.
source: ZeroID (helpnetsecurity) + GitGuardian overly broad scopes concern

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results; the only sources are a single HelpNetSecurity mention of ZeroID and a general GitGuardian concern — no named buyers, no funding data, no GitHub issues with engagement.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; multi-agent delegation is discussed broadly but no concrete shipping products or RFC timelines anchor a window.
rationale.fit: Delegation circuit, AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, and IdentityRegistry map directly with only minor modifications needed; the SDK stub already exists and cumulative-bit validation is built — this is almost a pure SDK-promotion task.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is ~10 days of SDK/test work reusing existing circuits and contracts, well within solo-founder capacity, though the delegate() stub promotion and chain builder add real integration surface area.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive — already enforces one-way scope narrowing (delegateeScope bitwise subset of delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, and chain linking up to 3 hops. Directly replaces RFC 8693 token exchange by proving scope constraints in ZK without revealing the full permission bitmask.

Each agent in the delegation chain proves its own credential validity (EdDSA signature, Merkle membership, expiry, cumulative-bit permissions) before delegation can occur. The selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask lets sub-agents prove they hold required permissions without revealing their full bitmask — the privacy advantage over plaintext JWT claims.

Root of every delegation chain is a human identity. The nullifier (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) ensures sybil resistance at the chain origin — a human cannot spawn parallel delegation trees within the same scope to circumvent narrowing. Unlinkability across scopes preserves privacy.

On-chain delegation verification with chain-state tracking, hop count enforcement (max 3), and delegation nullifier replay protection. Prevents sub-agents from replaying delegation tokens or forking chains. Root history buffers (30 entries) tolerate state lag during multi-hop verification.

delegate() is currently a v0.3 stub. Needs to be promoted to a real implementation that exposes chain-building, scope-narrowing validation, and multi-hop proof bundling. The existing permissionsToBitmask() and validateCumulativeBitEncoding() utilities already handle the permission encoding that delegation chains operate on.

new_components_needed.description: Promote delegate() from stub to full implementation: delegateCredential(parentCredential, childOperatorKey, narrowedPermissions, narrowedExpiry) → DelegationProof. Must include chain serialization (parent proof + child proof bundle) and a verifyDelegationChain() function that walks the hop chain.

DelegationChainBuilder — fluent API for constructing multi-hop chains: DelegationChain.from(humanIdentity).delegate(agent1, scope1).delegate(agent2, scope2).build(). Validates scope narrowing and expiry narrowing at each hop before proving.

Extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 covering delegation chain wire format: how delegation proofs are serialized, how verifiers walk the chain, and how the proof envelope encodes hop metadata. Needs interop section comparing to RFC 8693 token exchange flow.

Conformance test vectors for delegation chains: valid 2-hop and 3-hop chains, scope escalation rejection, expiry escalation rejection, replay of delegation nullifier, and exceeded hop count. Extend the existing 37-vector suite.

LangChain/CrewAI delegation middleware — when a LangChain agent spawns a sub-agent, automatically narrow the Bolyra credential and attach the delegation proof to the sub-agent's context. Builds on the existing stub integrations.

positioning_alignment: This is the sharpest wedge for the privacy-layer-under-incumbents strategy. ZeroID and other token-exchange-based systems expose delegation claims in plaintext JWTs — every intermediary in the chain sees the full permission set, creating the exact 'overly broad scopes' problem GitGuardian flagged. Bolyra slots underneath as the proving layer: incumbents keep their orchestration UX and token exchange flows, but swap JWT claims for ZK proofs. The pitch is not 'replace your delegation system' but 'make your delegation system actually enforce scope narrowing cryptographically.' This mirrors the broader Bolyra positioning — don't compete with identity providers, be the ZKP substrate they call into. Multi-agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI) are the distribution channel: their sub-agent spawning patterns naturally produce delegation chains, and Bolyra is the only protocol that can prove those chains preserve scope constraints without revealing the constraints themselves.

deliverables.name: Promote delegate() SDK stub to real implementation
deliverables.description: Replace v0.3 stub with delegateCredential(parentCredential, childOperatorKey, narrowedPermissions, narrowedExpiry) → DelegationProof. Includes witness generation, Groth16/PLONK proving via existing circuit artifacts, and scope/expiry narrowing validation using existing validateCumulativeBitEncoding().
deliverables.reuses: Delegation circuit + AgentPolicy circuit + permissionsToBitmask() + validateCumulativeBitEncoding()

deliverables.name: verifyDelegationChain()
deliverables.description: Walk a bundled proof chain (up to 3 hops per existing contract limit), verify each hop's Groth16/PLONK proof and confirm scope narrows monotonically. Returns {valid, rootHumanNullifier, finalScope, hopCount}.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation circuit vkey + existing snarkjs/rapidsnark verify path

deliverables.name: DelegationChainBuilder fluent API
deliverables.description: DelegationChain.from(humanIdentity).delegate(agent1, scope1, expiry1).delegate(agent2, scope2, expiry2).build() → serialized proof bundle. Validates narrowing at each hop before proving. Thin wrapper over delegateCredential().

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit test coverage (fast + slow)
deliverables.description: Extend existing test suite: 3-hop chain tests, scope escalation rejection, expiry widening rejection, replay protection via delegation nullifiers. Follow existing fast/slow split pattern.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry delegation chain verification
deliverables.description: Minor update to on-chain verifyDelegationChain — ensure hop count enforcement and delegation nullifier replay protection work end-to-end with the new SDK proof bundles. Regenerate Solidity verifier if circuit touched.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain wire format spec extension
deliverables.description: Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 Section on delegation: define DelegationProofBundle JSON schema, chain serialization order, and interaction with existing handshake nonce binding. Pure technical spec work.

deliverables.name: Python SDK bridge for delegation
deliverables.description: Extend subprocess bridge in sdk-python to expose delegate() and verifyDelegationChain() — thin shell that spawns Node, per existing pattern. No reimplementation of proving in Python.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain demo script
deliverables.description: CLI demo: human enrolls → delegates to orchestrator agent (READ+WRITE) → orchestrator sub-delegates to worker agent (READ only) → worker proves narrowed scope. Prints proof sizes and timing. No UI, no server.

risks: delegate() stub promotion may reveal hidden assumptions in circuit I/O wiring that require minor Delegation.circom changes — budget 1 extra day
3-hop chain proving time may be too slow for interactive use (~6s with snarkjs × 3 hops = 18s) — mitigate by documenting rapidsnark as production path and showing benchmark delta
Solidity verifier regeneration after any circuit change requires re-running trusted setup for Groth16 keys — PLONK path avoids this but has larger proof size
Solo founder bandwidth — 12 days assumes focused sprint with no context-switching to GeniusComply or ZKProva
mvp.success_criteria: npm run test:circuits:fast passes all delegation chain tests (1-hop, 2-hop, 3-hop, escalation rejection, expiry widening rejection); DelegationChainBuilder demo completes a 3-hop chain and verifies it both off-chain (SDK) and on-chain (Hardhat); wire format spec section passes editorial self-review against existing draft structure

title: Privacy-Preserving Agent Identity vs. Transparent DID/Signature Schemes
description: HUMAN Verified AI Agent and Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit both use Ed25519 signatures with public key registries, making every agent interaction linkable and traceable. Bolyra's nullifier-based approach lets agents prove valid credentials without revealing identity across sessions, solving the surveillance-by-default problem in current agent identity protocols.
source: HUMAN Verified AI Agent (humansecurity) + Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit

rationale.demand: Web search evidence completely failed with zero URLs; only two named projects (HUMAN Verified, Microsoft AGT) exist but no verifiable evidence that anyone is actively seeking a privacy-preserving alternative to their transparent approaches.
rationale.timing: No dated milestones, competitor launch dates, or standard deadlines were cited; the general existence of HUMAN and Microsoft projects suggests activity but provides zero timeline anchors to identify a window.
rationale.fit: Four named primitives map directly with the HumanUniqueness nullifier pattern ready to port to AgentPolicy, Delegation already provides transitive privacy, and the MVP extends existing circuits rather than building new ones.
rationale.feasibility: 8-day MVP is credible given it reuses the existing nullifier pattern and test harness, though verifier contract regeneration and on-chain event-emission review add integration risk.

mapped_primitives.usage: Already proves valid credentials (EdDSA operator signature, Merkle membership, permission bitmask) without revealing the agent's identity across sessions. The selective disclosure capability (requiredScopeMask) lets agents prove they hold specific permissions without exposing the full bitmask or linking interactions. This is the core primitive that directly addresses the 'surveillance-by-default' problem in HUMAN/Microsoft approaches.

The nullifier design (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) is the template for agent unlinkability. Agents currently lack per-scope nullifiers — they prove membership but their credential commitment is static across sessions, creating a linkability vector. Porting the human nullifier pattern to AgentPolicy would complete the privacy story.

Delegation chains preserve privacy transitively — a sub-agent proves it holds narrowed scope from a valid delegator without revealing the delegator's identity or the full chain. This is a direct counter to Microsoft's Agent Governance Toolkit which requires transparent delegation registries.

The dual LeanIMT trees and root history buffers allow membership verification without enumerating registered agents. The 30-entry root history buffer tolerates concurrent enrollments without breaking existing proofs. However, the on-chain handshake verification currently emits events that could be correlated — event emission policy needs review for privacy guarantees.

The SDK already exposes the privacy-preserving flow (proveHandshake/verifyHandshake) as the default path. No 'transparent mode' exists to accidentally leak identity. The error classes (StaleProofError, ScopeEscalationError) enforce privacy invariants at the API boundary.

The IETF draft already positions mutual ZKP auth as the alternative to transparent DID-based schemes. Section on threat model directly addresses linkability. Needs explicit comparison section against Ed25519-registry approaches to sharpen the positioning.

new_components_needed.description: Agent-scoped nullifier: add Poseidon2(scope, credentialCommitment) output to AgentPolicy so agents get per-scope unlinkability matching HumanUniqueness. Currently agents prove membership but lack nullifiers, meaning the same credential commitment appears across verifications.

Privacy comparison document: formal analysis showing linkability properties of Ed25519-registry schemes (HUMAN, Microsoft) vs Bolyra's nullifier approach. Quantify the privacy differential — number of bits leaked per interaction, correlation resistance under colluding verifiers.

Agent nullifier specification addendum to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth: define the agent nullifier derivation, scope binding semantics, and cross-verifier unlinkability guarantees. Needed before any integration partner can rely on the privacy claims.

Transparent-to-private bridge adapter: wrapper that accepts Ed25519-signed agent credentials (HUMAN/Microsoft format) and enrolls the agent into Bolyra's Merkle tree, giving existing agents a migration path to privacy-preserving proofs without re-credentialing from scratch.

Privacy mode configuration in SDK: explicit API for controlling nullifier scope selection, session isolation, and verifier-facing metadata minimization. Lets integrators tune the privacy/accountability tradeoff per use case.

positioning_alignment: This opportunity is the purest expression of Bolyra's 'privacy layer under incumbents' strategy. HUMAN and Microsoft have distribution (enterprise contracts, browser integration) but are architecturally locked into transparent identity. Bolyra doesn't compete on distribution — it offers the cryptographic primitive layer that these incumbents will eventually need when their customers demand privacy. The bridge adapter (accepting Ed25519 credentials into Merkle trees) is the specific technical wedge: incumbents' agents get privacy without abandoning their existing credential infrastructure, and Bolyra becomes the privacy substrate underneath. This mirrors the TLS-under-HTTP pattern — the transport layer wins by being invisible and indispensable.

deliverables.name: AgentPolicy v2 circuit with per-scope nullifier
deliverables.description: Add Poseidon2(scope, credentialCommitment) output signal to AgentPolicy.circom so agents get per-scope unlinkability matching HumanUniqueness. ~15 new constraint lines plus cumulative-bit compatibility check. Reuse existing Poseidon2 template and test harness.
deliverables.reuses: HumanUniqueness nullifier pattern + existing AgentPolicy circuit

deliverables.name: Verifier contract regeneration
deliverables.description: Regenerate Groth16 and PLONK verifier contracts from updated AgentPolicy zkey/vkey. Update IdentityRegistry to accept and verify the new nullifierHash public signal from agent proofs. Add event-emission review to avoid leaking correlatable data.

deliverables.name: SDK nullifier integration
deliverables.description: Update @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential and proveHandshake to generate and pass scope-bound nullifiers. Add agentNullifierHash to proof output type. Ensure verifyHandshake checks the new signal. No new public API methods—extends existing ones.

deliverables.name: Privacy comparison spec section
deliverables.description: Add Section 8 to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01: 'Comparison with Transparent Agent Identity Schemes'. Concrete table comparing Bolyra nullifier approach vs Ed25519-registry (HUMAN Verified) and transparent DID (Microsoft AGT) on linkability, selective disclosure, delegation privacy, and verifier complexity.

deliverables.name: Unlinkability test vectors
deliverables.description: Add conformance test vectors to spec/vectors/ proving: (1) same agent, different scopes produce different nullifiers, (2) same agent, same scope produces same nullifier (sybil resistance), (3) nullifier reveals nothing about credential commitment. Machine-verifiable JSON vectors.

deliverables.name: Privacy benchmark
deliverables.description: Run existing bench scripts (bench_agentpolicy.js) on updated circuit. Document constraint count delta, proving time delta (snarkjs vs rapidsnark), and verification gas cost delta. Publish as circuits/PRIVACY-BENCHMARK.md.

risks: AgentPolicy constraint count increase may push past pot16.ptau 2^16 limit—unlikely (current circuit is ~8K constraints, nullifier adds ~250) but must verify before trusted setup
Adding a public output signal to AgentPolicy is a breaking change to the proof interface—existing proofs become invalid, need migration path for any early adopters
Per-scope nullifier creates a new linkability surface within a single scope (intentional for sybil resistance) that must be clearly documented to avoid user confusion
Web search evidence failed so demand signal is unvalidated—this MVP is low-cost enough to proceed but should not be treated as validated demand
mvp.success_criteria: AgentPolicy v2 circuit compiles, passes all existing tests plus new nullifier-specific tests (unlinkability across scopes, linkability within scope, cumulative-bit compatibility). SDK proveHandshake produces proofs with agentNullifierHash that verify on-chain. Proving time regression under 10% vs baseline. Conformance vectors pass the spec runner. Comparison section reviewed for technical accuracy against published HUMAN and Microsoft specs.

title: Standardized Cumulative-Bit Permission Encoding for Agent Scopes
description: AgentIDs uses scoped JWT tokens and Microsoft uses IATP, but neither enforces permission hierarchies cryptographically. Bolyra's 8-bit cumulative encoding with circuit-enforced implication rules (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM implies FINANCIAL_SMALL) could become the interop standard for agent permission scoping, especially for financial services where scope escalation is a regulatory concern.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were provided; demand is purely inferred from the existence of AgentIDs and Microsoft IATP without any verifiable engagement metrics, named buyers, or funding data.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited with specific dates; the 18-month-plus assessment from evidence gathering confirms no closing window exists.
rationale.fit: AgentPolicy circuit already implements the exact 8-bit cumulative encoding with selective disclosure, Delegation enforces scope narrowing on-chain, and the SDK already exports the reference functions — this is extracting what already exists into a standalone spec.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is well-scoped (extracting existing code into a standalone package and writing a spec), low technical risk, but standards adoption without proven demand is speculative investment.

mapped_primitives.usage: Already implements the exact 8-bit cumulative encoding with circuit-enforced implication rules. The selective disclosure capability (proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing full bitmask) is the core differentiator versus JWT-based scope strings. This circuit IS the reference implementation of the proposed standard.

Enforces scope narrowing (delegateeScope must be bitwise subset of delegatorScope) across delegation chains. This is the composability layer that makes cumulative-bit encoding useful beyond single-hop — sub-agents provably cannot escalate permissions. Critical for the financial services regulatory story.

On-chain delegation verification with hop count enforcement provides an auditable trail that scope narrowing was cryptographically enforced, not just policy-asserted. The delegation nullifier replay protection prevents reuse of narrowed credentials after revocation.

permissionsToBitmask() and validateCumulativeBitEncoding() are already the SDK-level API for the standard. These become the reference SDK that other implementations test against. The structured error classes (InvalidPermissionError, ScopeEscalationError) already model the failure modes the standard needs to define.

37 existing conformance vectors provide a starting point, but the standard needs dedicated permission-encoding test vectors covering all implication rule edge cases (e.g., setting bit 4 without bits 2+3, scope narrowing across delegation hops, expiry interaction with scope).

new_components_needed.description: Standalone IETF internet-draft (draft-bolyra-cumulative-bit-permissions-00) defining the encoding format, implication rules, scope narrowing algebra, and interop with existing scope string formats (OAuth 2.0 RFC 6749 scope, AgentIDs scoped JWTs). Must be protocol-agnostic — usable with or without ZKP enforcement.

Permission encoding test vector suite (separate from existing 37 vectors): canonical encoding/decoding, implication rule validation, scope narrowing truth tables, invalid encoding rejection cases. Target 50+ vectors covering all 8 bits and their interactions.

Encoding-only utility package (@bolyra/permissions or similar) that implements cumulative-bit validation without requiring the full ZKP stack. Lets AgentIDs/IATP adopters use the encoding standard without buying into Bolyra's proving infrastructure. Trojan horse for later ZKP adoption.

AgentIDs scope-string-to-bitmask adapter: bidirectional mapping between AgentIDs JWT scope strings and Bolyra cumulative-bit encoding, with validation that the mapping preserves implication semantics. Demonstrates interop without requiring AgentIDs to change their wire format.

IATP (Microsoft Identity and Trust Protocol) credential-to-bitmask adapter: maps IATP permission claims to cumulative-bit encoding for verification, enabling Bolyra to sit as a cryptographic enforcement layer beneath Microsoft's governance toolkit.

positioning_alignment: This is textbook privacy-layer-under-incumbents. AgentIDs and Microsoft IATP both need permission scoping but implement it as policy assertions (JWT claims, governance rules) with no cryptographic enforcement. Bolyra doesn't compete with their identity layer — it slides underneath as the enforcement layer that makes their permission claims verifiable. The standalone encoding standard is the wedge: adopt the bit format (zero switching cost), then discover that ZKP enforcement is the only way to make scope narrowing non-repudiable for financial regulators. The financial services angle (GENIUS Act, NCUA) creates urgency that pure-tech standards processes lack — credit unions need provable scope controls before their examiners ask for them, and neither AgentIDs nor IATP can provide cryptographic guarantees today.

deliverables.name: draft-bolyra-cumulative-bit-permissions-00
deliverables.description: Standalone IETF-style internet-draft defining the 8-bit cumulative encoding format, implication rules, scope narrowing algebra, and a mapping table to OAuth 2.0 scope strings. Protocol-agnostic — no ZKP dependency in the core spec. Includes a non-normative appendix showing ZKP enforcement via selective disclosure.
deliverables.reuses: existing AgentPolicy circuit logic + sdk/permissions.ts validation rules extracted into prose

deliverables.name: permission-encoding-test-vectors.json
deliverables.description: 50+ test vectors covering: canonical encode/decode for all 8 bits, implication rule enforcement (setting bit 4 without 2+3 must fail), scope narrowing truth tables across 1-hop and 2-hop delegation, expiry interaction with scope, invalid encoding rejection. Machine-readable JSON with expected pass/fail and error codes.
deliverables.reuses: existing 37 conformance vectors as structural template + validateCumulativeBitEncoding() as oracle

deliverables.name: @bolyra/permissions standalone package
deliverables.description: Zero-dependency TypeScript package (~200 LOC) extracting permissionsToBitmask(), validateCumulativeBitEncoding(), narrowScope(), and isSubsetScope() from @bolyra/sdk. No ZKP, no snarkjs, no circuits — pure bit arithmetic. Publishable to npm independently so non-Bolyra projects can adopt the encoding without buying the full stack.
deliverables.reuses: sdk/permissions.ts — direct extraction with minor API cleanup

deliverables.name: Conformance runner for permission vectors
deliverables.description: CLI script that loads permission-encoding-test-vectors.json and runs each vector against @bolyra/permissions, reporting pass/fail. Other implementations can swap in their own library and run the same vectors. Extends existing spec/conformance-runner/ pattern.

deliverables.name: OAuth scope string mapping
deliverables.description: Reference table and utility function mapping cumulative-bit values to equivalent OAuth 2.0 scope strings (e.g., bit 2 → 'financial:small') and AgentIDs scoped JWT claim format. Shows interop path for existing ecosystems without requiring migration.

risks: Standards without adoption are shelf-ware — no signal yet that AgentIDs or Microsoft would reference this encoding over their own scope formats
IETF internet-drafts require a sponsoring working group or individual submission track; solo-authored drafts rarely gain traction without co-authors from other organizations
The 8-bit field width is adequate for Phase 1 but may need extension for real-world financial services (PCI scopes, SOX controls) — premature standardization risk
Web search evidence returned nothing, meaning there is zero validated external demand for this specific encoding format right now
Extracting @bolyra/permissions as standalone may create a maintenance burden if the core SDK evolves the encoding
mvp.success_criteria: The MVP succeeds if: (1) @bolyra/permissions is published on npm and passes all 50+ test vectors, (2) the spec draft is posted as an individual internet-draft or pre-print that can be cited, (3) at least one external project (AgentIDs, Veramo, or a CrewAI/LangChain plugin) references the encoding or runs the conformance vectors within 90 days of publication, and (4) the spec is picked up in at least one agent-identity community discussion (W3C CCG, DIF, or IETF OAuth WG mailing list).

title: ZKP Alternative to OAuth-Like Agent Passport Flows
description: Agent Passport offers 'Sign in with Google, but for Agents' using standard OAuth flows. Bolyra differentiates by proving agent identity without a centralized identity provider—no single point of trust, no token revocation race conditions, and cryptographic scope enforcement rather than policy-based access control.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence failed entirely and the only source is a single HN thread—no named buyers, no funding data, no independent URLs, putting this squarely in anecdotal territory.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the evidence assessment itself says 18+ months, and Agent Passport's maturity and market traction are unverified.
rationale.fit: Four existing primitives (AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation, IdentityRegistry) map directly with zero or minor modifications, and the SDK already exposes the core proveHandshake/verifyHandshake flow that replaces OAuth token exchange.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is well-scoped around specs and thin SDK wrappers over existing primitives, but the OAuth-bridge spec and Express middleware add integration surface area that needs real-world validation against actual OAuth-consuming services.

mapped_primitives.usage: Proves agent identity and permissions without a centralized IdP. The EdDSA operator signature + credential commitment replaces OAuth client_id/client_secret. Selective disclosure of permission bits replaces scope strings in OAuth tokens. Expiry enforcement replaces token TTL without needing a revocation endpoint.

Proves the human behind the agent without revealing identity—replaces the 'resource owner' in OAuth. Nullifier prevents sybil attacks (one human can't spawn unlimited agent identities within a scope) while remaining unlinkable across services.

Enables the human→agent→sub-agent chain that Agent Passport models as nested OAuth delegations. Scope narrowing enforced in-circuit replaces policy-based scope reduction. No token chain to intercept or replay.

On-chain root anchoring replaces the centralized registry that Agent Passport relies on. Atomic handshake verification means a relying party can validate both human backing and agent credential in one call—no round-trip to an authorization server.

SDK's proveHandshake/verifyHandshake is the direct replacement for Agent Passport's OAuth token exchange. A service accepting agent requests calls verifyHandshake instead of introspecting a bearer token.

new_components_needed.description: OAuth-to-Bolyra bridge spec: defines how a relying party currently expecting OAuth bearer tokens can accept a Bolyra proof as an alternative credential format (e.g., HTTP header encoding, proof-in-DPoP-style binding). Maps OAuth scopes to Bolyra permission bitmask.

Discovery/negotiation layer: agents need to advertise 'I support Bolyra ZKP auth' to services that may also accept OAuth. Similar to RFC 9449 DPoP negotiation but for ZKP proofs. Likely a well-known endpoint or HTTP WWW-Authenticate challenge extension.

Agent Passport compatibility shim: a middleware that translates Bolyra proofs into short-lived OAuth tokens for services not yet ZKP-native. Allows incremental adoption without requiring all relying parties to upgrade simultaneously.

Reputation anchor (optional): on-chain record of successful handshakes that builds agent reputation over time without linking individual sessions. Enables 'trusted agent' status without centralized scoring.

positioning_alignment: Agent Passport positions itself as the 'Sign in with Google for agents'—inherently centralized, creating a new identity chokepoint. Bolyra positions as the privacy layer that sits underneath: operators can use Agent Passport as a convenience layer while Bolyra provides the cryptographic proof substrate. This is the 'picks and shovels' play—when Agent Passport or any competitor becomes the dominant OAuth-style provider, Bolyra is the verifiable credential layer they build on rather than competing with. The no-revocation-needed property (proofs are self-contained with expiry) eliminates the distributed systems nightmare of token revocation at scale, which becomes Agent Passport's Achilles heel as agent-to-agent traffic grows.

deliverables.name: OAuth-to-Bolyra scope mapping spec
deliverables.description: RFC-style document mapping standard OAuth 2.0 scopes (openid, profile, email, plus custom scopes) to Bolyra 8-bit permission bitmask. Defines canonical encoding so any relying party can translate between the two worlds. Includes a registry of well-known scope-to-bit mappings.

deliverables.name: ZKP-DPoP HTTP binding spec
deliverables.description: Defines how a Bolyra proof travels in HTTP requests as an alternative to Bearer tokens. Extends the DPoP (RFC 9449) pattern: proof goes in a Bolyra-Proof header, bound to the request URI and method. Defines WWW-Authenticate challenge with scheme 'BolyraZKP' so servers can advertise support. Agents negotiate by attempting ZKP auth first, falling back to OAuth.

deliverables.name: SDK: HTTP proof binding helpers
deliverables.description: Add to @bolyra/sdk: createProofHeader(proof, method, uri) that serializes a handshake proof into the Bolyra-Proof header format. parseProofHeader(headerValue) for server-side extraction. verifyBoundProof(header, method, uri) that checks proof validity AND request binding. Thin wrappers over existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake.

deliverables.name: Express.js middleware
deliverables.description: bolyra-express middleware: drop-in replacement for passport-oauth2. Reads Bolyra-Proof header, calls verifyBoundProof, populates req.agent with verified identity fields (nullifier, permissions bitmask, expiry). Falls through to next auth middleware if no Bolyra-Proof header present, enabling dual-stack OAuth+ZKP on the same endpoint.

deliverables.name: Side-by-side demo
deliverables.description: Single-page demo with two panes: left shows a standard OAuth agent auth flow (redirect, token exchange, bearer token, introspection roundtrip); right shows Bolyra flow (generate proof locally, attach header, verify in one call, no roundtrip). Hardcoded scenario: agent requests FINANCIAL_SMALL permission from a mock merchant API. Shows proof generation time, verification time, and zero network calls to an IdP.

deliverables.name: Comparison blog post
deliverables.description: Technical blog post: 'Agent Auth Without an Identity Provider — How ZKP Replaces OAuth for Autonomous Agents'. Covers the trust model difference (centralized IdP vs cryptographic proof), token revocation vs expiry-in-circuit, scope strings vs cumulative-bit enforcement. References the HN Agent Passport discussion. Links to the spec and demo.

risks: No web search evidence of demand — the opportunity is based on a single HN discussion; real adoption signal is unvalidated
OAuth compatibility shim (the high-complexity new component) is deliberately excluded from this MVP to stay within solo capacity, but without it incremental adoption is harder
ZKP proof generation latency (~1-2s even with rapidsnark) may not meet latency expectations of services used to sub-100ms OAuth token introspection
The 'negotiate ZKP vs OAuth' pattern adds complexity for relying parties — they may just stick with OAuth rather than support two auth schemes
Agent Passport or similar projects could adopt ZKP themselves, neutralizing the differentiation before Bolyra gains traction
mvp.success_criteria: Three signals within 60 days of publishing: (1) at least 2 external GitHub issues or PRs on the express middleware from developers trying to integrate it, (2) the blog post generates measurable discussion (>20 HN points or >5 substantive comments on the protocol design), (3) at least 1 agent framework (LangChain, CrewAI, or similar) maintainer expresses interest in supporting the Bolyra-Proof header in their HTTP client layer. If none of these occur, the opportunity lacks real pull and should be deprioritized.

title: LangChain/Framework-Native ZKP Auth Middleware
description: LangChain's agent authorization explainer signals framework-level demand for authentication primitives. Bolyra's existing LangChain integration path (integrations/langchain/) positions it to offer drop-in middleware that replaces API-key-based auth with ZKP handshakes, giving framework users cryptographic guarantees without protocol expertise.

rationale.demand: Web search evidence returned zero results and no URLs were provided — the only signal is a single LangChain blog post with no engagement metrics, named buyers, or funding data.
rationale.timing: No competitor launch dates, standard milestones, or regulatory deadlines were cited; the general awareness that frameworks are discussing auth does not constitute an anchored timeline.
rationale.fit: Strong primitive mapping — AgentPolicy, HumanUniqueness, Delegation, and the existing integrations/langchain/ stub map directly with only minor modifications needed, and ZKP provides clear advantage over API-key auth for scope enforcement.
rationale.feasibility: MVP is well-scoped at ~12 days reusing existing circuits and SDK, though the langchain-bolyra-tool stub requires major work and LangChain's callback API stability is an external dependency risk.

mapped_primitives.usage: LangChain agents prove they hold valid operator-signed credentials with specific permission scopes before executing tools — the AgentPolicy circuit generates the proof that replaces API key presentation

Human-in-the-loop approval flows use HumanUniqueness proofs to authenticate the operator/user who authorized the agent, enabling cryptographic audit trails back to a unique human without revealing identity

Multi-agent chains (e.g. orchestrator delegates to sub-agents) use the Delegation circuit to prove scope narrowing — a retrieval agent gets READ_DATA only, a payment agent gets FINANCIAL_SMALL only — enforced at the ZKP level, not just framework config
On-chain verification endpoint for high-value tool calls (financial ops, PII access) where the LangChain middleware needs a trust anchor beyond local verification
The middleware wraps createAgentCredential + proveHandshake into LangChain's RunnableConfig/callback system so developers call familiar framework patterns while ZKP mechanics are abstracted
Existing stub in integrations/langchain/ becomes the actual middleware package — already has the correct package boundary and import path reserved

new_components_needed.description: BolyraAuthMiddleware — a LangChain Runnable that intercepts tool invocations, checks cached proofs or generates fresh AgentPolicy proofs, and attaches proof metadata to the RunnableConfig. Implements LangChain's BaseCallbackHandler for automatic injection into chains.

BolyraToolGuard — a wrapper that decorates any BaseTool with permission requirements (requiredScopeMask), rejecting calls where the agent's proven bitmask doesn't satisfy the tool's minimum permissions
Credential caching layer — middleware needs session-scoped proof caching (proofs valid for N seconds per nonce) to avoid re-proving on every tool call within a chain execution

Framework Auth Profile in the IETF draft — a new section specifying how framework middleware maps LangChain/CrewAI tool permissions to Bolyra's 8-bit permission model, including canonical scope-to-bitmask mappings for common tool categories (retrieval, mutation, financial, PII)
CrewAI parallel adapter — once LangChain middleware ships, a thin CrewAI wrapper (integrations/crewai/) that maps CrewAI's tool decorator pattern to the same BolyraToolGuard interface

positioning_alignment: This is the canonical privacy-layer-under-incumbents play: LangChain has 80K+ GitHub stars and massive framework adoption but zero cryptographic auth primitives — they explicitly punt to 'API keys or OAuth'. Bolyra slots in as invisible infrastructure that framework developers adopt via a single middleware import, not a protocol migration. The middleware pattern means LangChain/CrewAI never need to understand ZKPs — they just get 'this agent is authorized for these permissions' as a boolean gate. This creates distribution through incumbents (framework ecosystem) while the protocol layer remains Bolyra-controlled. Once middleware is adopted, switching costs are high because proofs are bound to Bolyra's circuit artifacts and registry contracts.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/langchain-auth
deliverables.description: BolyraAuthMiddleware — a LangChain RunnableCallable that intercepts tool invocations via BaseCallbackHandler, checks a session-scoped proof cache, and generates/attaches AgentPolicy proofs to RunnableConfig. Drop-in: chain.with_config(bolyra_auth(credential)).

deliverables.name: BolyraToolGuard
deliverables.description: Decorator function that wraps any BaseTool with a requiredScopeMask. Rejects calls where the agent's proven cumulative bitmask doesn't satisfy the tool's minimum permissions. ~150 lines leveraging existing validateCumulativeBitEncoding().

deliverables.name: Credential proof cache
deliverables.description: Session-scoped LRU cache keyed by (credentialHash, nonce, scopeMask) with configurable TTL (default 60s). Avoids re-proving on every tool call within a single chain execution. Added to @bolyra/sdk as ProofSessionCache class.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain helper
deliverables.description: delegateToSubAgent() utility that wraps the Delegation circuit for multi-agent LangChain chains — orchestrator narrows scope before spawning retrieval/payment sub-agents. Thin wrapper over existing proveHandshake + Delegation.

deliverables.name: LangChain middleware example
deliverables.description: Working example: RAG agent with 3 tools (search=READ_DATA, write=WRITE_DATA, pay=FINANCIAL_SMALL), each guarded by BolyraToolGuard. Shows credential creation, middleware injection, and proof verification in ~80 lines.

deliverables.name: Framework Auth Profile spec section
deliverables.description: New section in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01.md specifying how framework middleware maps tool permissions to scope bitmasks, proof lifecycle within chain execution, and caching semantics.

deliverables.name: README + quickstart
deliverables.description: Package README with install, 5-line quickstart, architecture diagram, and API reference for the middleware and ToolGuard.

risks: LangChain's callback/runnable API changes frequently — middleware may break on minor version bumps
Proof generation latency (~200ms with rapidsnark) may feel slow in interactive agent loops without the cache
Adoption depends on LangChain ecosystem actually needing auth beyond API keys — blog signal is not confirmed demand
Web search evidence returned nothing — demand signal is weak/unvalidated
Framework lock-in: building for LangChain specifically may not transfer to CrewAI/AutoGen without additional adapters
mvp.success_criteria: A developer can npm install @bolyra/langchain-auth, wrap an existing LangChain agent with 3 lines of config, and have every tool call cryptographically gated by ZKP-proven permissions — verified by: (1) the demo runs end-to-end with real proofs, (2) unauthorized tool calls are rejected with clear errors, (3) sub-agent delegation narrows scope correctly, (4) at least 2 external developers can follow the quickstart without assistance within 15 minutes.
